title: Dן¿½s Aliter Visum; Or, Le Byron De Nos Jours
content:
I.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stop, let me have the truth of that!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is that all true? I say, the day
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ten years ago when both of us
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Met on a morning, friends as thus
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We meet this evening, friends or what?

II.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did you because I took your arm
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sillily smiled, ן¿½A mass of brass
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That sea looks, blazing underneath!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ While up the cliff-road edged with heath,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We took the turns nor came to harm

III.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did you consider ן¿½Now makes twice
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That I have seen her, walked and talked
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With this poor pretty thoughtful thing,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Whose worth I weigh: she tries to sing;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Draws, hopes in time the eye grows nice;

IV.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Reads verse and thinks she understands;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Loves all, at any rate, thatן¿½s great,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Good, beautiful; but much as we
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Down at the bath-house love the sea,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Who breathe its salt and bruise its sands:

V.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½While . . . do but follow the fishing-gull
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½That flaps and floats from wave to cave!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thereן¿½s the sea-lover, fair my friend!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½What then? Be patient, mark and mend!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Had you the making of your scull?ן¿½

VI.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And did you, when we faced the church
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With spire and sad slate roof, aloof
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From human fellowship so far,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where a few graveyard crosses are,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And garlands for the swallowsן¿½ perch,

VII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did you determine, as we stepped
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oן¿½er the lone stone fence, ן¿½Let me get
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Her for myself, and whatן¿½s the earth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With all its art, verse, music, worth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Compared with love, found, gained, and kept?

VIII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Schumannן¿½s our music-maker now;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Has his march-movement youth and mouth?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ingresן¿½s the modern man that paints;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Which will lean on me, of his saints?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Heine for songs; for kisses, how?ן¿½

IX.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And did you, when we entered, reached
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The votive frigate, soft aloft
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Riding on air this hundred years,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Safe-smiling at old hopes and fears,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Did you draw profit while she preached?

X.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Resolving, ן¿½Fools we wise men grow!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Yes, I could easily blurt out curt
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Some question that might find reply
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As prompt in her stopped lips, dropped eye,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And rush of red to cheek and brow:

XI.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Thus were a match made, sure and fast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Mid the blue weed-flowers round the mound
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Where, issuing, we shall stand and stay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For one more look at baths and bay,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sands, sea-gulls, and the old church last

XII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½A match ן¿½twixt me, bent, wigged and lamed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Famous, however, for verse and worse,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sure of the Fortieth spare Arm-chair
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½When gout and glory seat me there,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½So, one whose love-freaks pass unblamed,

XIII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And this young beauty, round and sound
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½As a mountain-apple, youth and truth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With loves and doves, at all events
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½With money in the Three per Cents;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Whose choice of me would seem profound:

XIV.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½She might take me as I take her.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Perfect the hour would pass, alas!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Climb high, love high, what matter? Still,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Feet, feelings, must descend the hill:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½An hourן¿½s perfection canן¿½t recur.

XV.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Then follows Paris and full time
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½For both to reason: ן¿½Thus with us!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Sheן¿½ll sigh, ן¿½Thus girls give body and soul
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½At first word, think they gain the goal,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½When ן¿½t is the starting-place they climb!

XVI.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½My friend makes verse and gets renown;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Have they all fifty years, his peers?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½He knows the world, firm, quiet and gay;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Boys will become as much one day:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Theyן¿½re fools; he cheats, with beard less brown.

XVII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½For boys say, Love one or I die!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½He did not say, The truth is, youth
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½I want, who am old and know too much;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Iן¿½d catch youth: lend one sight and touch!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Drop heartן¿½s blood where lifeן¿½s wheels grate dry!

XVIII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½While I should make rejoinderן¿½ (then
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ It was, no doubt, you ceased that least
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Light pressure of my arm in yours)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½I can conceive of cheaper cures
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½For a yawning-fit oן¿½er books and men.

XIX.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½What? All I am, was, and might be,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½All, books taught, art brought, lifeן¿½s whole strife,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Painful results since precious, just
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½Were fitly exchanged, in wise disgust,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½For two cheeks freshened by youth and sea?

XX.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½All for a nosegay! what came first;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½With fields on flower, untried each side;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½I rally, need my books and men,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½And find a nosegayן¿½: drop it, then,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½ן¿½No match yet made for best or worst!ן¿½

XXI.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That ended me. You judged the porch
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We left by, Norman; took our look
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At sea and sky; wondered so few
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Find out the place for air and view;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Remarked the sun began to scorch;

XXII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Descended, soon regained the baths,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And then, good-bye! Years ten since then:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ten years! We meet: you tell me, now,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By a window-seat for that cliff-brow,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On carpet-stripes for those sand-paths.

XXIII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Now I may speak: you fool, for all
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your lore! WHO made things plain in vain?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What was the sea for? What, the grey
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sad church, that solitary day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Crosses and graves and swallowsן¿½ call?

XXIV.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was there nought better than to enjoy?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No feat which, done, would make time break
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And let us pent-up creatures through
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Into eternity, our due?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No forcing earth teach heavenן¿½s employ?

XXV.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No wise beginning, here and now,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ What cannot grow complete (earthן¿½s feat)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And heaven must finish, there and then?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No tasting earthן¿½s true food for men,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Its sweet in sad, its sad in sweet?

XXVI.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ No grasping at love, gaining a share
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oן¿½ the sole spark from Godן¿½s life at strife
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With death, so, sure of range above
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The limits here? For us and love,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Failure; but, when God fails, despair.

XXVII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ This you call wisdom? Thus you add
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Good unto good again, in vain?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You loved, with body worn and weak;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I loved, with faculties to seek:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Were both loves worthless since ill-clad?

XXVIII.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Let the mere star-fish in his vault
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Crawl in a wash of weed, indeed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rose-jacynth to the finger-tips:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He, whole in body and soul, outstrips
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Man, found with either in default.

XXIX.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But whatן¿½s whole, can increase no more,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Is dwarfed and dies, since hereן¿½s its sphere.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The devil laughed at you in his sleeve!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You knew not? That I well believe;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or you had saved two souls: nay, four.

XXX.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For Stephanie sprained last night her wrist,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ankle or something. ן¿½Pooh,ן¿½ cry you?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At any rate she danced, all say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vilely; her vogue has had its day.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here comes my husband from his whist.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Robert Browning represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Robert Browning's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
